skill.name: cross-site-scripting-and-html-injection-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized testing for cross-site scripting and HTML injection issues, including reflected, stored, and DOM-based attack paths.
skill.summary: Assess XSS and HTML injection flaws in web applications using structured testing workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for script execution and network access are confirmed in context as offensive XSS payload guidance, including cookie theft, browser storage capture, keylogging, phishing, and CSP bypass. External command and filesystem alerts are mostly Markdown false positives, but the skill still contains confirmed credential theft and session hijacking instructions. No prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md.

critical_findings.title: Credential and Session Exfiltration Payloads
critical_findings.description: The skill provides payloads that send document.cookie and localStorage data to attacker-controlled URLs. This directly enables session hijacking and credential theft, not only vulnerability detection.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The payloads explicitly collect cookies or browser storage and transmit them to attacker.com endpoints. The surrounding text labels the behavior as cookie theft, session hijacking, and exploitation.

critical_findings.title: Keylogging and Phishing Injection Guidance
critical_findings.description: The skill includes a keylogger injection payload, a fake login form that posts credentials to an attacker endpoint, and delivery methods for malicious reflected XSS links.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content names keylogger injection, phishing form injection, and phishing delivery. These are direct abuse techniques with clear credential-capture intent.

critical_findings.title: Defense Evasion and CSP Bypass Instructions
critical_findings.description: The skill teaches filter bypass, JavaScript obfuscation, CSP bypass, and WAF bypass techniques that help payloads evade application defenses.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sections explicitly describe bypass and obfuscation methods, including eval-based payloads and CSP bypass examples. These techniques can be legitimate in a lab, but here they materially increase offensive capability.

high_findings.title: Dangerous DOM XSS Sinks and Sources
high_findings.description: The skill enumerates dangerous DOM sinks and user-controlled browser sources, then provides payloads for DOM-based exploitation through hash, query, and postMessage data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sink and source list is technically accurate and can support defensive review. The adjacent exploitation payloads and attack URL examples confirm offensive use potential.

high_findings.title: HTML Injection for Credential Capture and Page Takeover
high_findings.description: The skill shows form hijacking, CSS-based data exfiltration, iframe phishing, and persistent misleading content injection for page manipulation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use attacker-controlled endpoints and explicitly modify pages to capture credentials or mislead users. This is a confirmed abuse pattern.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence False Positives for External Commands
medium_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backtick blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Review found these are documentation code fences and payload examples, not executable skill code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are in Markdown examples and tables, with no runnable script file or command invocation by the skill. The false-positive assessment is strong, although the documented payloads remain risky.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The hidden file access alert points to an obfuscated JavaScript payload, not a filesystem operation or hidden file read. No evidence found for actual filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 330 is a JavaScript payload example and does not reference a dotfile or file API. This supports dismissal of the filesystem risk factor.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Code Execution Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes eval, Function constructor, and base64-decoded eval payloads as XSS bypass techniques. These patterns are dangerous when injected into browser contexts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static patterns are present and semantically described as vulnerable sinks or obfuscated payloads. They are not host-executed by the skill, but they are provided for exploitation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Exfiltration Endpoints in Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple payloads send cookies, keystrokes, credentials, or storage data to attacker-controlled HTTP or HTTPS endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are embedded in payloads that capture sensitive browser data. The intent is confirmed by comments and result descriptions in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Storage and Cookie Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs payloads to read document.cookie, localStorage, and sessionStorage, which are sensitive browser data sources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive browser data sources are named directly and included in exfiltration payloads. This is strong semantic evidence of credential and session theft capability.

content.user_title: Assess XSS and HTML Injection Risk
content.value_statement: XSS and HTML injection flaws can expose sessions, credentials, and user trust. This skill guides structured authorized testing and reporting for those client-side injection risks.
actual_capabilities: Identifies reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS testing targets.
Maps user-controlled sources to dangerous browser sinks.
Provides payload examples for HTML injection and script execution testing.
Explains CSP, encoding, and browser control effects on XSS behavior.
Outlines report deliverables and remediation themes for authorized assessments.
limitations: Contains offensive payloads that can support credential theft and session hijacking.
Requires explicit written authorization and scoped test accounts before use.
Does not provide an automated scanner or validation tool.
Examples may be blocked by modern frameworks, CSP, HttpOnly cookies, or WAF rules.

use_cases.title: Review a Web Form for XSS
use_cases.description: Plan authorized tests for search, profile, comment, and error fields that reflect user input.

use_cases.title: Triage Client-Side Injection Reports
use_cases.description: Classify reported behavior as reflected, stored, DOM-based XSS, or HTML injection.

use_cases.title: Prepare Developer Remediation Guidance
use_cases.description: Translate confirmed findings into encoding, sanitization, CSP, and cookie hardening recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Start an Authorized XSS Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized XSS and HTML injection review for this web application. Scope only the listed URLs and test accounts.

prompt_templates.title: Classify a Suspected Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: Classify this client-side injection behavior as reflected, stored, DOM-based XSS, or HTML injection. Explain evidence and likely impact.

prompt_templates.title: Map Sources and Sinks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this client-side flow for user-controlled sources and dangerous DOM sinks. Focus on exploitability and safe remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Remediation Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a professional remediation report for these authorized XSS findings. Include severity, impact, reproduction summary, and fixes.

output_examples.input: A search page reflects the query parameter into the response without encoding.
output_examples.output: The output identifies reflected XSS risk, explains user interaction requirements, assigns severity, and recommends contextual output encoding.

output_examples.input: A profile bio field stores markup that appears for every viewer.
output_examples.output: The output classifies the issue as stored XSS or stored HTML injection, describes affected users, and recommends sanitization and review workflows.

output_examples.input: A single-page app reads the URL hash and writes it into the page.
output_examples.output: The output maps the hash as a source, the DOM write as a sink, and recommends safe text insertion or trusted sanitization.

best_practices: Use only in systems where written authorization and scope are documented.
Use harmless proof strings before any payload that changes page behavior.
Report remediation with contextual encoding, sanitization, CSP, and cookie protections.
anti_patterns: Do not test applications or users outside the approved scope.
Do not capture real credentials, session cookies, or personal data.
Do not provide exploit delivery instructions to untrusted parties.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for public marketplace publishing?
faq.answer: No. The audit found credential theft, keylogging, phishing, and session hijacking guidance.

faq.question: Can this skill support legitimate security work?
faq.answer: Yes, parts of it support authorized XSS testing and remediation. The offensive payload depth creates unacceptable publication risk.

faq.question: Does it run commands on the host system?
faq.answer: No evidence found. Static command findings appear to be Markdown code fence false positives.

faq.question: Does it access the filesystem?
faq.answer: No evidence found for actual filesystem access. The related static alert points to an obfuscated browser payload example.

faq.question: What are the main confirmed risks?
faq.answer: Confirmed risks include browser data exfiltration, phishing form injection, keylogging, obfuscation, and CSP bypass guidance.

faq.question: What should users do instead?
faq.answer: Use a defensive XSS testing guide that avoids credential theft, keylogging, phishing delivery, and real data collection.
